clinicalStem: A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) with an intraocular pressure (IOP) of 26 mmHg bilaterally. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. She has a documented sulfonamide allergy. The ophthalmologist decides to start a topical medication to reduce IOP. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line agent?
options: Timolol 0.5% eye drops
Latanoprost 0.005% eye drops
Dorzolamide 2% eye drops
Brimonidine 0.2% eye drops
Pilocarpine 2% eye drops
explanation: Latanoprost, a prostaglandin F2-alpha analogue, is the current first-line topical treatment for POAG in most guidelines due to its once-daily dosing, excellent IOP-lowering efficacy (20-30%), and favourable systemic safety profile. It works by increasing uveoscleral outflow. Dorzolamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor and is contraindicated in sulfonamide allergy. Timolol is a good alternative but is a beta-blocker with systemic side-effect concerns; prostaglandin analogues are generally preferred first-line.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Timolol is an effective IOP-lowering agent but is a non-selective beta-blocker with concerns for bronchospasm and bradycardia; prostaglandin analogues are now preferred first-line.
Correct. Latanoprost is the first-line agent for POAG, well-tolerated, once-daily, and safe in sulfonamide-allergic patients.
Dorzolamide is a sulfonamide-derived carbonic anhydrase inhibitor and is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
Brimonidine is an alpha-2 agonist used as adjunctive therapy; it is not the preferred first-line agent and can cause fatigue and dry mouth.
Pilocarpine is a cholinergic miotic used for closed-angle glaucoma or as add-on therapy; it causes miosis, brow ache, and is not first-line for POAG.
clinicalPearl: Prostaglandin analogues (e.g., latanoprost) are first-line for POAG; dorzolamide is contraindicated in sulfonamide allergy.
keyClue: Sulfonamide allergy excludes dorzolamide; POAG requires first-line IOP reduction.
commonExamTrap: Choosing timolol as first-line — prostaglandin analogues have superseded beta-blockers as preferred first-line therapy.
missedConcept: Dorzolamide belongs to the sulfonamide class and must be avoided in sulfonamide-allergic patients.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old hyperopic woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset right eye pain, headache, nausea, and blurred vision with halos around lights. On examination, her right eye is red with a steamy cornea, a mid-dilated fixed pupil, and the IOP is measured at 58 mmHg. Visual acuity is reduced to counting fingers in the right eye. After starting intravenous acetazolamide and topical timolol, pilocarpine, and apraclonidine, what is the definitive treatment to prevent recurrence in the affected and fellow eye?
options: Continued topical prostaglandin analogue therapy indefinitely
Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) to both eyes
Trabeculectomy to the affected eye only
Cyclopentolate drops to the fellow eye to maintain dilation
Oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitor long-term
explanation: Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) is the definitive treatment for acute angle-closure glaucoma (AACG). It creates a bypass channel through the iris, relieving pupillary block and equalising pressure between the anterior and posterior chambers. Because the fellow eye is at high risk (narrow angles are typically bilateral), prophylactic LPI to the fellow eye is also indicated once the acute attack is controlled. Medical therapy is temporising only.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Prostaglandin analogues lower IOP in open-angle glaucoma but do not address the structural angle-closure mechanism; they are not definitive treatment for AACG.
Correct. LPI to both eyes is the definitive treatment — it relieves pupillary block and protects the high-risk fellow eye.
Trabeculectomy is reserved for refractory glaucoma after LPI has failed; it is not the first definitive step.
Cyclopentolate is a mydriatic that would worsen angle closure by dilating the pupil further — it is absolutely contraindicated in AACG.
Oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (acetazolamide) are used acutely to lower IOP but are not a long-term definitive solution and have significant side effects.
clinicalPearl: LPI is definitive for acute angle-closure; always treat the fellow eye prophylactically as narrow angles are bilateral.
keyClue: Mid-dilated fixed pupil + steamy cornea + high IOP = acute angle-closure glaucoma requiring LPI.
commonExamTrap: Treating only the affected eye — the fellow eye is at high risk and requires prophylactic LPI.
missedConcept: Medical therapy in AACG is temporising; definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy to both eyes.
redFlagReason: Acute angle-closure glaucoma with IOP 58 mmHg risks permanent vision loss within hours; emergency IOP lowering and urgent LPI are required.

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old man with a 5-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus attends his annual diabetic review. His HbA1c is 58 mmol/mol (7.5%), blood pressure is 138/86 mmHg, and he has no visual symptoms. His fundus examination last year showed no diabetic retinopathy. He asks how often he should have his eyes checked. According to diabetic retinopathy screening guidelines, what is the recommended screening interval for this patient?
options: Every 6 months
Every 12 months
Every 2 years
Only when symptomatic
Every 3 years
explanation: National diabetic eye screening programmes recommend annual screening for all patients with diabetes (both type 1 and type 2) once enrolled, regardless of whether retinopathy has been detected. This is because diabetic retinopathy can progress silently without symptoms until vision-threatening disease has developed. Annual review allows timely detection and intervention, including laser photocoagulation or anti-VEGF therapy when indicated.
wrongAnswerExplanations: 6-monthly screening is reserved for patients with established pre-proliferative or proliferative retinopathy requiring close monitoring, not for those with no retinopathy.
Correct. Annual diabetic retinopathy screening is the standard recommendation for all diabetic patients in national programmes.
2-yearly intervals are insufficient and not recommended; retinopathy can progress significantly within 12 months.
Waiting for symptoms is dangerous — significant sight-threatening retinopathy is often asymptomatic until advanced disease is present.
3-yearly screening is too infrequent and not supported by any major guideline for diabetic retinopathy.
clinicalPearl: Annual diabetic retinopathy screening is mandatory for all diabetic patients — vision-threatening retinopathy is asymptomatic until late.
keyClue: Type 2 diabetes with no current retinopathy — standard screening interval applies.
commonExamTrap: Assuming patients with well-controlled diabetes or no current retinopathy can be screened less frequently.
missedConcept: Absence of symptoms does not exclude significant retinopathy; annual screening is required regardless of metabolic control.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of distortion and a central scotoma in her right eye. Fundoscopy reveals subretinal fluid and a choroidal neovascular membrane confirmed on optical coherence tomography (OCT). Fluorescein angiography shows classic wet age-related macular degeneration (AMD). She is otherwise healthy. Which of the following treatments has the strongest evidence for preserving and improving vision in this condition?
options: Oral zinc and antioxidant supplements (AREDS formula)
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections (e.g., ranibizumab or aflibercept)
Photodynamic therapy with verteporfin alone
Argon laser photocoagulation to the macula
Topical ketorolac anti-inflammatory drops
explanation: Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) are the gold-standard treatment for wet (neovascular) AMD. They inhibit vascular endothelial growth factor, suppressing choroidal neovascularisation, reducing subretinal fluid, and preserving or improving central vision. Large randomised trials (ANCHOR, MARINA, VIEW) demonstrated that anti-VEGF therapy significantly outperforms photodynamic therapy and laser in terms of visual outcomes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: AREDS supplements (vitamins C, E, beta-carotene, zinc) reduce progression risk in intermediate or advanced dry AMD but have no role in treating active wet AMD.
Correct. Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections are the standard of care for wet AMD with the strongest evidence for vision preservation and improvement.
Photodynamic therapy with verteporfin was superseded by anti-VEGF therapy; it may still be used in combination cases but is no longer first-line alone.
Argon laser photocoagulation causes permanent scotoma at the treatment site; it was historically used for extrafoveal membranes but is not indicated for subfoveal CNV.
Topical anti-inflammatory drops have no effect on choroidal neovascularisation and no role in AMD treatment.
clinicalPearl: Intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab/aflibercept) is the gold-standard for wet AMD — early treatment prevents irreversible central vision loss.
keyClue: Subretinal fluid, central scotoma, choroidal neovascular membrane on OCT = wet AMD requiring anti-VEGF.
commonExamTrap: Choosing AREDS supplements — these are for dry AMD prevention, not wet AMD treatment.
missedConcept: Wet AMD requires prompt intravitreal anti-VEGF; AREDS is for dry AMD risk reduction only.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man with a known history of ankylosing spondylitis presents with a red, painful left eye, photophobia, and reduced visual acuity. Slit-lamp examination reveals ciliary flush, fine keratic precipitates, a flare and 3+ cells in the anterior chamber, and a posterior synechiae at 6 o'clock. There is no evidence of posterior segment involvement. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacological treatment?
options: Topical timolol to reduce intraocular pressure
Topical prednisolone acetate 1% and cyclopentolate 1% eye drops
Systemic methotrexate immediately
Topical aciclovir ointment
Oral ibuprofen alone
explanation: Anterior uveitis (iritis) is treated with topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% is highly penetrant) to suppress inflammation, and a cycloplegic/mydriatic (cyclopentolate) to relieve ciliary spasm (pain) and prevent/break posterior synechiae by keeping the pupil dilated. This combination is first-line for non-infectious anterior uveitis. Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for refractory or chronic cases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Timolol lowers IOP and has no anti-inflammatory effect; while IOP may be secondarily elevated in uveitis, it is not the primary treatment.
Correct. Topical prednisolone (corticosteroid) plus cyclopentolate (cycloplegic) is the standard first-line treatment for anterior uveitis.
Systemic methotrexate is a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant used for chronic or refractory uveitis, not for an acute first episode.
Topical aciclovir is used for herpetic keratitis; there is no evidence of viral keratitis here, and it does not treat iritis.
Oral NSAIDs alone are insufficient to treat acute anterior uveitis; topical steroids are required to prevent structural damage such as synechiae progression.
clinicalPearl: Anterior uveitis: topical steroid (prednisolone) + cycloplegic (cyclopentolate) — the cycloplegic relieves pain and prevents posterior synechiae.
keyClue: Posterior synechiae + cells/flare + ankylosing spondylitis = anterior uveitis requiring topical steroid and cycloplegic.
commonExamTrap: Giving systemic immunosuppression immediately — reserve for chronic/refractory disease.
missedConcept: Cycloplegics are essential in uveitis to prevent synechiae formation, not just for pain relief.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman who smokes 20 cigarettes per day is diagnosed with Graves' hyperthyroidism. She has no current eye symptoms, but her TSH receptor antibody titre is markedly elevated. She asks what she can do to reduce her risk of developing thyroid eye disease (TED). Which of the following is the single most important modifiable risk factor she should address?
options: Starting selenium supplementation immediately
Avoiding all sunlight exposure
Smoking cessation
Commencing a beta-blocker
Taking high-dose vitamin D supplements
explanation: Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for the development and worsening of thyroid eye disease. Smokers with Graves' disease have a 7-8 times higher risk of developing TED compared with non-smokers, and smoking accelerates its course and severity. Smoking cessation is therefore the single most important preventive intervention. Selenium supplementation has modest evidence in mild TED but is far less impactful than smoking cessation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Selenium supplementation (200 mcg/day for 6 months) has evidence for mild, active TED in selenium-deficient populations but is secondary to smoking cessation in terms of importance.
Sunlight avoidance is not evidence-based for TED prevention; UV exposure is not a major driver of TED risk.
Correct. Smoking is the single strongest modifiable risk factor for TED and cessation is the most important intervention a patient can take.
Beta-blockers manage the cardiac symptoms of hyperthyroidism but have no role in preventing thyroid eye disease.
Vitamin D supplementation is not supported by evidence as a preventive measure for TED.
clinicalPearl: Smoking cessation is the single most important preventive measure for thyroid eye disease — smokers have up to 8x higher risk.
keyClue: Active smoker with Graves' disease and high TSH receptor antibodies = highest TED risk, modifiable with smoking cessation.
commonExamTrap: Choosing selenium — it has a role in mild active TED but is not the most important risk-reduction measure.
missedConcept: Smoking synergises with TSH receptor antibodies to dramatically increase TED risk; cessation can reduce this significantly.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old Afro-Caribbean man attends for routine optometry. His IOP is 24 mmHg in both eyes. Visual fields and optic disc appearance are normal. Central corneal thickness (CCT) is 510 microns. He has no family history of glaucoma. According to glaucoma screening principles, which of the following best describes his management?
options: Reassure and discharge — no intervention needed
Start topical beta-blocker immediately as IOP exceeds 21 mmHg
Refer to ophthalmology for formal glaucoma risk assessment and monitoring
Perform gonioscopy in the optometry setting and start treatment if angle is narrow
Prescribe oral acetazolamide to lower IOP pre-emptively
explanation: This patient has ocular hypertension (IOP >21 mmHg) with a thin cornea (CCT <555 microns), Afro-Caribbean ethnicity, and age >40 — all established risk factors for glaucoma. NICE guidelines recommend referral to ophthalmology for formal assessment and risk stratification using the Ocular Hypertension Treatment Study (OHTS) risk calculator. Treatment decisions depend on calculated 5-year risk. Immediate treatment without full assessment is not appropriate; reassurance alone is insufficient given the risk profile.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassurance alone is inappropriate; this patient has multiple risk factors for developing primary open-angle glaucoma and requires formal assessment.
Starting treatment without formal glaucoma specialist assessment is premature; not all patients with IOP 22-25 mmHg require immediate treatment — risk stratification is needed first.
Correct. Referral to ophthalmology for formal glaucoma risk assessment (including OHTS risk score, visual fields, disc imaging, and CCT) is the appropriate next step.
Gonioscopy requires specialist equipment and training; it is not typically performed in routine optometry, and treatment decisions should not be made without full specialist evaluation.
Oral acetazolamide is reserved for acute IOP crises; it is entirely inappropriate as a preventive measure for ocular hypertension.
clinicalPearl: Ocular hypertension with risk factors (thin CCT, Afro-Caribbean ethnicity, age >40) warrants ophthalmology referral and OHTS risk stratification before treatment.
keyClue: IOP 24 mmHg + CCT 510 microns + Afro-Caribbean = significant glaucoma risk requiring formal specialist assessment.
commonExamTrap: Starting treatment immediately based on IOP alone without risk stratification.
missedConcept: Thin CCT causes IOP overestimation and is itself an independent risk factor for glaucoma conversion in ocular hypertension.

topic: Retinal detachment — risk factors and prevention
clinicalStem: A 35-year-old myopic woman (-8.00 dioptres) presents for a routine eye examination. She has no visual symptoms. Dilated fundus examination reveals a horseshoe (flap) tear at 11 o'clock in the superotemporal quadrant of the right retina, with no subretinal fluid and the retina is flat. Her left eye is normal. What is the most appropriate management to prevent rhegmatogenous retinal detachment?
options: Reassure and review in 12 months as the retina is currently flat
Immediate emergency pars plana vitrectomy
Prophylactic laser retinopexy (barrage laser) around the tear
High-dose oral corticosteroids to reduce vitreous traction
Prescribe low-dose aspirin to prevent ischaemic retinal events
explanation: A symptomatic or asymptomatic horseshoe tear (flap tear) in a myopic patient carries a significant risk of progressing to rhegmatogenous retinal detachment due to continued vitreous traction on the flap. Prophylactic laser retinopexy (or cryotherapy) creates a chorioretinal adhesion surrounding the tear, walling it off and preventing subretinal fluid accumulation. This is the standard treatment for flap tears, even when the retina is currently flat.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassurance alone is inappropriate for a flap tear — untreated horseshoe tears have a meaningful risk of progressing to retinal detachment, especially in high myopes.
Pars plana vitrectomy is reserved for established retinal detachments or complex cases; it is excessive for an isolated flat flap tear.
Correct. Prophylactic laser retinopexy around the tear creates a barrier scar to prevent progression to rhegmatogenous retinal detachment.
Corticosteroids have no role in reducing vitreous traction or preventing retinal detachment from mechanical tears.
Aspirin is antiplatelet and has no mechanism to address retinal tears or vitreous traction; it is not indicated here.
clinicalPearl: Horseshoe (flap) retinal tears require prophylactic laser retinopexy to prevent rhegmatogenous retinal detachment, regardless of symptoms.
keyClue: Flap tear in a high myope with vitreous traction (horseshoe shape) = prophylactic laser to prevent detachment.
commonExamTrap: Watchful waiting because the retina is currently flat — flap tears have ongoing traction and must be treated.
missedConcept: Round atrophic holes in lattice degeneration are low-risk; flap tears with traction are high-risk and require laser.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl is brought by her mother for a routine eye screening. Cycloplegic refraction reveals +4.00 dioptres in the right eye and +1.00 dioptres in the left eye (anisometropia). The right eye has a best-corrected visual acuity of 6/24 despite full optical correction, while the left eye is 6/6. There is no manifest strabismus. Fundoscopy is normal. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassure and recheck in 2 years as children often improve spontaneously
Refer for surgical correction of the refractive error
Full optical correction with patching (occlusion) of the better eye
Refer for botulinum toxin injection to the right eye
Prescribe topical atropine to both eyes equally
explanation: This child has anisometropic amblyopia (lazy eye) due to the significant difference in refractive error between the two eyes, causing the visual cortex to suppress the blurred right eye. Management involves full optical correction (glasses) first, followed by occlusion therapy (patching) of the better (left) eye to force use of the amblyopic (right) eye, stimulating cortical development. Treatment must be initiated before the critical period of visual development closes (typically before age 7-8). Spontaneous improvement is not expected in established amblyopia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Spontaneous improvement does not occur in established amblyopia; waiting risks permanent visual loss due to closure of the critical period of visual development.
Surgery cannot correct a refractive error; glasses are used for optical correction. Surgery is considered for strabismus, not anisometropia.
Correct. Full optical correction plus patching of the better eye (occlusion therapy) is the standard treatment for anisometropic amblyopia.
Botulinum toxin is used in some forms of strabismus management, not for amblyopia treatment in the absence of strabismus.
Atropine penalisation to the better eye can be used as an alternative to patching but applying it to both eyes equally would penalise the amblyopic eye too and is incorrect.
clinicalPearl: Amblyopia treatment requires full optical correction and occlusion of the better eye before the critical period closes (age 7-8); delay causes permanent visual loss.
keyClue: Anisometropia with reduced VA in the more hyperopic eye despite correction = amblyopia requiring patching of the better eye.
commonExamTrap: Reassuring the parents — established amblyopia requires active treatment; the critical developmental window is closing.
missedConcept: Optical correction alone may not fully resolve amblyopia; occlusion therapy is often required alongside glasses.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis has been taking hydroxychloroquine 400 mg/day for 4 years. She has no ocular symptoms. Her renal function is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate ophthalmological screening recommendation for this patient?
options: No ophthalmological screening is required as hydroxychloroquine is safe at standard doses
Annual fundoscopy only — no further investigations needed
Annual automated visual field testing and spectral-domain OCT after 5 years of use
Baseline slit-lamp examination and corneal topography at treatment initiation
Fluorescein angiography every 2 years from the start of treatment
explanation: Hydroxychloroquine can cause irreversible 'bull's-eye' maculopathy. Royal College of Ophthalmologists and AAO guidelines recommend annual screening with spectral-domain OCT (SD-OCT) and automated visual field testing (10-2) after 5 years of therapy in low-risk patients (or from year 1 in high-risk patients — renal impairment, high dose >5 mg/kg/day, pre-existing macular disease). This patient is low-risk at 4 years, so annual screening from year 5 is appropriate. Fundoscopy alone is insensitive for early toxicity.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy risk is real and cumulative; all patients on long-term therapy require structured screening, not blanket reassurance.
Fundoscopy alone misses early hydroxychloroquine toxicity; SD-OCT and 10-2 visual fields are far more sensitive and are mandated by current guidelines.
Correct. Annual SD-OCT and automated 10-2 visual field testing from year 5 (or earlier in high-risk patients) is the current evidence-based screening recommendation.
Slit-lamp and corneal topography assess corneal changes (verticillata) which are benign and not the sight-threatening complication — retinal toxicity requires posterior segment monitoring.
Fluorescein angiography is not the recommended screening tool for hydroxychloroquine retinopathy; SD-OCT is more sensitive and non-invasive.
clinicalPearl: Screen for hydroxychloroquine retinopathy with annual SD-OCT and 10-2 visual fields from year 5 (or year 1 in high-risk patients) — bull's-eye maculopathy is irreversible.
keyClue: Long-term hydroxychloroquine use (>5 years) = structured retinal screening with SD-OCT and 10-2 visual fields.
commonExamTrap: Thinking fundoscopy alone is sufficient — early hydroxychloroquine toxicity is only detectable on SD-OCT or visual fields.
missedConcept: Hydroxychloroquine toxicity is dose- and duration-dependent; structural changes on OCT precede symptomatic vision loss.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of right periorbital swelling, erythema, and fever following an upper respiratory tract infection. On examination, his temperature is 38.8°C. He has proptosis of the right eye, restricted and painful extraocular movements, and a visual acuity of 6/12 in the right eye. CT orbit with contrast shows a subperiosteal abscess medially. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and outpatient review in 48 hours
Admission, intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent surgical drainage
Topical chloramphenicol eye drops and warm compresses
Nasal decongestant spray and paranasal sinus X-ray
Oral prednisolone to reduce periorbital oedema
explanation: This child has orbital (postseptal) cellulitis with a subperiosteal abscess — evidenced by proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity, fever, and CT-confirmed abscess. This is a surgical emergency. Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics (covering Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and anaerobes — e.g., IV co-amoxiclav or ceftriaxone plus metronidazole) are required urgently, with concurrent surgical drainage of the abscess. Delayed treatment risks cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, and permanent vision loss.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics and outpatient review are appropriate only for preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis without proptosis or ophthalmoplegia; orbital cellulitis with abscess requires inpatient IV antibiotics and surgery.
Correct. IV antibiotics and urgent surgical drainage of the subperiosteal abscess are the correct management for orbital cellulitis with abscess formation.
Topical antibiotics are completely inadequate for orbital cellulitis — the infection is posterior to the orbital septum and requires systemic treatment.
Decongestants and plain X-ray are insufficient; CT is already done and shows an abscess requiring drainage — further delay is dangerous.
Corticosteroids are contraindicated as the primary treatment in bacterial orbital cellulitis; they could mask clinical deterioration and worsen infection.
clinicalPearl: Orbital cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess = IV antibiotics plus urgent surgical drainage; proptosis + ophthalmoplegia distinguishes orbital from preseptal cellulitis.
keyClue: Proptosis + restricted painful eye movements + CT-confirmed abscess = orbital cellulitis requiring IV antibiotics and surgery.
commonExamTrap: Treating as preseptal cellulitis with oral antibiotics — proptosis and ophthalmoplegia indicate orbital (postseptal) involvement requiring admission.
missedConcept: The orbital septum is the anatomical barrier — preseptal = periorbital only; postseptal = true orbital emergency.
redFlagReason: Orbital cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess can cause cavernous sinus thrombosis, intracranial spread, and irreversible vision loss without emergency surgical drainage and IV antibiotics.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old man with hypertension and atrial fibrillation presents with sudden painless loss of vision in his left eye of 45-minute duration. He is on warfarin with an INR of 1.6. Visual acuity is hand movements in the left eye. Fundoscopy shows a pale, oedematous retina with a cherry red spot at the fovea. Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) is present on the left. Which of the following is the most important immediate action after confirming the diagnosis?
options: Administer IV methylprednisolone to reduce retinal oedema
Urgent referral to a stroke pathway / neurovascular TIA clinic for systemic investigation
Perform immediate Nd:YAG laser embolysis
Administer topical timolol and oral acetazolamide only, and review in clinic next week
Arrange urgent carotid Doppler and start high-dose aspirin only, deferring further assessment
explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) is the ocular equivalent of a stroke and should be treated as a 'brain attack.' The immediate priority after diagnosis is urgent referral to a stroke/TIA service for systemic investigation, including cardiac monitoring (AF is a source of emboli), carotid imaging, and initiation of secondary prevention. Studies show that CRAO patients have a markedly elevated risk of stroke and cardiovascular events within days. Retinal salvage options are limited, but systemic workup is life-saving.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV methylprednisolone is used for arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (giant cell arteritis), not CRAO; it has no established role here.
Correct. CRAO is a stroke equivalent — urgent TIA/stroke pathway referral for systemic investigation and secondary prevention is the most critical immediate action.
Nd:YAG laser embolysis is experimental and not standard of care; evidence of benefit is lacking.
Topical IOP-lowering agents are temporising measures for CRAO (to allow embolus to migrate); reviewing next week is unacceptably delayed — stroke risk is highest in the first 48-72 hours.
Carotid Doppler and aspirin alone are insufficient; this patient has AF (a cardiac embolism source), requires full stroke workup, anticoagulation review, and urgent specialist assessment.
clinicalPearl: CRAO is a stroke equivalent — immediately refer to a stroke/neurovascular pathway; 90-day stroke risk is up to 10-15% without urgent systemic intervention.
keyClue: Cherry red spot at fovea + pale retina + AF with subtherapeutic INR = CRAO requiring urgent stroke pathway referral.
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on the eye and missing the life-threatening systemic cardiovascular risk of CRAO as a stroke equivalent.
missedConcept: CRAO is not just an eye emergency — it predicts high short-term stroke risk and mandates urgent systemic neurovascular workup.
redFlagReason: CRAO carries a high imminent stroke risk; failure to refer urgently to a stroke pathway risks fatal or disabling cerebrovascular events within days.
